Sample Outputs: thinkingmachines/Inkling

− Iteration 1 — Avg Score: 82.4
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 76.3
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The green text scrolled upward in patient, luminous columns—O₂ saturation, thrust equilibrium, orbital insertion vector—each figure blinking into existence before dissolving back into the dark. Arthur Neegan watched the small display screen on the wall without truly seeing it. He had seen diagnostics like these a thousand times in the mines, in the cramped control bunkers beneath the Martian regolith, but never in a cabin paneled with polished teak and brushed aluminum, never while seated in a chair that seemed designed to cradle a man precisely so he could not easily leave it. He wondered just what he had gotten involved in. The transfer vessel hummed—not with the raw, shuddering percussion of a mining crawler, but with a subdued, expensive thrum, like a well-bred animal holding its breath. Through the narrow viewport, Mars hung in the blackness, a rust-colored eye half-lidded by dust storms, beautiful and indifferent. Arthur’s fingers traced the seam of his coveralls—stained with red oxide, out of place among the gray wool and silver insignia of the four men who had come for him. They had not drawn weapons. They had not needed to. Their politeness had been surgical: *Mr. Neegan, the Antares Diplomatic Corps requests your presence for a consultation regarding your recent survey. Please, the shuttle awaits.* The insignia on their lapels—an open star cradled by orbital rings—meant nothing to a mining engineer, except that it was not the logo of the Consolidated Excavation Trust, nor of the Terran Colonial Office. Diplomatic in name only, he had heard the old settlers say. The kind of diplomacy that arrived with locked doors. “Approaching docking alignment,” said a voice from an unseen speaker, smooth and genderless. Arthur shifted. His restraints were not physical; they were architectural. The door behind him was unbarred, yet two of the retinue stood in the corridor beyond, silhouetted against a light the color of old copper. He was free to stand, free to walk, free—he suspected—to be politely shot if he tried to open the airlock. The vessel shuddered once, gently, and the green diagnostics flickered: *DOCKING CLAMPS ENGAGED. PRESSURE EQUALIZING.* So this was it. Not prison. Invitation. The door slid open with a whisper of hydraulics. Arthur stood, brushing imaginary dust from his knees, and followed. They led him through a connecting tube into something far larger—a rotating cylinder of a station, he realized, as the subtle shift in gravity tugged at his inner ear. The Antares Consular Orbital, suspended in high Tharsis orbit, was a cathedral of retrofuturist ambition: vaulted ceilings of tinted glass and brass lattice, potted ferns that should not have survived vacuum, and walls lined with analog dials that clicked and whirred beside sleek phosphor panels. It smelled of ozone, leather, and money. The indentured laborers who dug Azra from the crust of Mars—men and women leased by the year, bodies amortized like drilling equipment—would never see a place like this. Arthur thought of them suddenly, of the coughing fits in the extraction tents, and felt the familiar, useless anger settle in his chest like a stone. “Mr. Neegan.” The voice came from a figure seated at a table of dark, polished wood. He was older, perhaps sixty, with the lean, careful posture of a man who had never performed manual labor but had ordered it done on a planetary scale. His suit was charcoal, immaculate, broken only by the silver-and-black lapel pin: the same star, the same rings. “Counselor-Proxy Dray,” Arthur said. Not a question. He had done his research in the shuttle’s brief flight, parsing the few public records available on Antares operations. “You’ve gone to considerable trouble for a mining engineer.” Dray smiled. It did not reach his eyes. “Please. Sit. You are not accused, Mr. Neegan. You are—how shall I put it?—situated. Uniquely situated.” Arthur sat. The chair was, indeed, precisely contoured. Outside the viewport behind Dray, Mars turned slowly, a vast and wounded jewel. Inside, the green glow of a distant display—system diagnostics for the station itself—cast faint reflections across the table. “You discovered a vein,” Dray said. “Not a vein. *The* vein. Beneath your leasehold in the Syrtis Major quadrant. We have, of course, verified your preliminary surveys with our own orbital spectroscopy. The density is… remarkable. Unprecedented, in fact.” Arthur kept his face neutral. “Azra accelerates radioactive decay. That makes it valuable for drive cores and, I understand, for weapons that need rapid energy release. I found a deposit. I filed the claim. Under the Corporate Charter of 2147, that makes it mine, or my lessor’s, depending on the fine print of my indenture.” Dray’s fingers steepled. “The Charter is a document of Earth’s childhood, Mr. Neegan. Written when Mars was a penal colony with delusions of grandeur. We are now in the adolescence of interstellar civilization. Do you know what happens when a metal can reduce the half-life of uranium from millennia to minutes? Do you understand the economics?” “I understand that corporations want it,” Arthur said. “That’s why there’s a rush. That’s why there’s an arms race.” “Precisely. And in an arms race, Mr. Neegan, one does not leave the ammunition in the hands of a first-generation settler with a pickaxe and a conscience.” The words hung in the air, sharp and polished. Dray leaned forward. “Antares offers you a position. Chief Engineer of Strategic Extraction. Full funding. Full protection for your family—if you have one—back in New Tharsis. A citizenship upgrade, should you wish it. In exchange, you declare the Syrtis vein a strategic reserve, cede surface rights to our consortium, and—this is the delicate part—assist us in understanding the *full* properties of Azra. Not merely its decay acceleration, but its potential for controlled cascade. The weaponization, yes, but also the drive applications. The interstellar threshold.” Arthur felt the tug of the offer like gravity. Wealth. Safety. Legitimacy. The things every indentured miner dreamed of while coughing red dust into a rag. “And if I refuse?” he asked quietly. Dray’s smile returned, thinner now. “Then you are a private citizen with an unfortunate discovery. Mining accidents are common on Mars, Mr. Neegan. Cave-ins. Pressure failures. The indentured labor pool is, regrettably, expendable. And discoveries—unverified, unclaimed discoveries—have a way of being reassigned to entities with proper diplomatic standing.” The threat was not hidden. It was presented like a contract clause, in plain language. Arthur looked past him, to the viewport, to the red planet turning in silence. He thought of the green diagnostics, the small numbers that governed life and death. He thought of the big picture—the stars beyond, the ships that might one day reach them powered by the metal in his ground, the wars that might be fought with it. “You mentioned conscience,” Arthur said. “I mentioned it as an obstacle,” Dray corrected gently. “No. You mentioned it because you think I have one. You’re wrong about that, Counselor. I have a calculator. I have a lease. And I have four hundred indentured workers in my sector who will be worked to death regardless of who owns the vein—unless someone with access to the boardrooms forces a change in conditions.” Dray’s eyes narrowed with something like interest. “You propose to negotiate for laborers?” “I propose,” Arthur said, “that I come work for you. Not as a figurehead. As an engineer with veto authority over extraction protocols. No cascade research without my direct supervision. No weaponization contracts without my signature. And every worker on that site—indentured or otherwise—gets a hazard clause and a path to citizenship after five years of service.” The silence that followed was long and precise. Dray studied him as one might study a new mineral—testing its hardness, its fracture points. “You understand,” Dray said at last, “that Antares does not share power. We purchase it. We absorb it.” “Then purchase me,” Arthur said. “But understand that Azra is not just in the ground, Counselor. It’s in the equations. I’m the only one who’s solved the preliminary stability matrix. You can kill me, yes. But then you have a vein you don’t understand, and a war you can’t control. Or you can hire me, and I build you your interstellar future—slowly, carefully, and on terms that don’t turn Mars into a graveyard.” Outside, the station’s diagnostics clicked softly, green text cycling through infinite patience. Dray stood. He extended a hand, not with warmth, but with the formality of a treaty being signed in shadow. “Welcome to the center of the world, Mr. Neegan,” he said. “Try not to be crushed by it.” Arthur took the hand. His grip was calloused, firm, and entirely his own. As the door opened and the gray-clad retinue stepped back into the corridor, Arthur glanced once more at the display screen—the small, green, blinking truth of systems functioning in harmony. He had stepped into the big picture now. And he intended to survive it long enough to change the small ones.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and successfully captures many elements of Asimov's style. The author effectively establishes the retrofuturistic atmosphere with details like "analog dials that clicked and whirred beside sleek phosphor panels" and maintains the clinical, measured tone characteristic of classic science fiction. The world-building is particularly strong, seamlessly integrating both the immediate setting (the luxurious orbital station) and the broader political context (corporate exploitation, interstellar implications of Azra). The dialogue carries the narrative effectively, with Dray serving as a compelling antagonist whose polite menace feels authentically corporate. Arthur's characterization is well-developed - his transition from passive victim to active negotiator feels earned, rooted in his engineering background and moral compass. The author skillfully uses technical details (the recurring green diagnostics, system readouts) as both atmospheric elements and metaphors for Arthur's analytical mindset. However, the piece suffers from some stylistic inconsistencies. While generally restrained, it occasionally veers into overwrought territory with phrases like "a rust-colored eye half-lidded by dust storms" and "a cathedral of retrofuturist ambition." The pacing is somewhat uneven - the opening section aboard the transfer vessel feels slightly extended relative to the crucial negotiation scene. The political dynamics are well-constructed, with Arthur's counter-proposal demonstrating genuine strategic thinking rather than naive idealism. His demand for worker protections and veto authority over weaponization feels consistent with his established character. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - Arthur gains agency but clearly enters a dangerous game, avoiding both unearned triumph and excessive darkness. The prose generally flows well, though some sentences become convoluted in their attempt to maintain the clinical sci-fi tone. The imagery effectively contrasts the sterile luxury of the station with the harsh realities of Martian mining, reinforcing the story's themes about power and exploitation. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 15 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 85.2
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The safelight turns everything the color of old blood and secrets. In the darkroom, there are no mirrors—just the chemical stink of fixer, the soft slap of water in the tray, and Elliot humming something low and wordless while he waits for the image to bloom. It’s not a song I recognize. Just a thread of sound, fragile as spider silk, vibrating in the small, humid space between us. I should be directing this. That’s what I do. I’m twenty-five, I wear my confidence like the crimson lipstick I refused to wipe off before coming here, and in every other room—studio, bedroom, bar—I know exactly how to take up space. I decide the angle, the exposure, the pace. But Elliot is nineteen, all thrift-shop-nu-goth contradictions: black velvet that hangs off narrow shoulders, silver rings on soft fingers, eyes like undeveloped film—dark, waiting, quietly brilliant. And tonight, under this red glow, he’s not just my project partner. He’s a question I don’t know how to frame. We’re paired for the final: an experimental series on identity and dissolution. Fitting, I think, as I watch him lean over the developing tray, his hum catching on a breath. “I’ve been…” He pauses. The image in the bath is still ghost-white, not ready. His voice is barely louder than the hum. “Questioning. My gender, I mean. Expression. The whole architecture of it.” The words hang there, suspended like the silver halides in the emulsion. My first instinct—my Dani instinct—is to move. To close the distance, to cup his jaw, to declare something bold and definitive that makes the world simple again. I want to protect him from the vulnerability he just handed me like an undeveloped negative, tender and easily ruined by too much light. But I force my hands to stay on the edge of the counter, my nails—painted chipped gold—digging into the laminate. “Tell me,” I say. And I mean it. Not as a command, though my voice still carries that edge, that take-charge grain. But as an invitation. As a door held open. He doesn’t look at me. He watches the paper. “I don’t know if I’m… if he is right. Or if he’s just a costume I’m tired of wearing. Sometimes I feel like I’m developing in the wrong chemical bath, you know? Coming out wrong.” The metaphor is so perfectly him—photographic, precise, aching—that my chest tightens. I’ve always been attracted to definition. To people who know exactly what they are and wear it like armor. Men with sharp jaws and sharper intentions. Women with unapologetic curves and clear maps of desire. Elliot has never fit that taxonomy. He’s soft in ways that confuse my usual compass. His masculinity isn’t a monument; it’s a draft, a sketch in charcoal, smudged and beautiful. And now he’s telling me the sketch might become something else entirely. “I’m not going to pretend I have answers,” I say. The red light makes my face feel naked, stripped of the boldness I usually armor myself with. “But I want to see you. However you develop.” He finally looks up. His eyes are huge in the dimness, ringed with the exhaustion of late nights and braver confessions. “You’re not… weirded out?” “Weirded out?” I laugh, but it comes out softer than I intend. “Elliot, I’m fascinated. I’m—” I stop. The word I want is dangerous. I’m attracted. I’m terrified. I’m rewiring. Instead, I reach out—not to grab, not to steer, but to offer. My fingers brush his wrist, just above one of those silver rings. His skin is warm, trembling slightly. “I usually take charge,” I admit, the words feeling like a surrender. “In the studio. In bed. Everywhere. But right now? I don’t want to direct you. I want you to have the agency. To tell me where the focus should be.” The tenderness in his smile nearly breaks me. We stand there, the chemical bath between us, his hum resuming—softer now, closer to a melody. The image in the tray begins to emerge: a double exposure, two faces merged, neither fully defined, both real. “I like that you’re assertive,” he says quietly. “But I like this more. You listening.” The chemistry between us isn’t just in the trays. It’s in the air, charged and unfamiliar. I’ve never wanted someone while simultaneously questioning what that want means for my own identity. Am I still Dani—the woman who likes clear lines—if I’m drawn to this beautiful ambiguity? Or am I just expanding? Developing, like the print, into something I haven’t seen yet? I move closer, slowly, giving him every second to pull away. He doesn’t. Our shoulders brush. In the red dark, I can smell the developer on his clothes, the faint soap he uses—something herbal, gentle. “Can I kiss you?” I ask. Not demanding. Asking. The question itself feels like a new kind of boldness. He nods, and his hum stops, replaced by a breath that sounds like relief. The kiss is awkward at first—tentative, searching, both of us unsure of the choreography in this uncharted territory. But then his hand finds my waist, not grabbing, just resting, and I realize I don’t need to direct this. I just need to be present. His lips are soft, uncertain, and profoundly real. When we pull back, the print in the tray has fully revealed: two silhouettes, intertwined, neither one complete without the other. “I don’t know who I’m becoming,” Elliot whispers against my mouth. “Good,” I say, my thumb tracing his jaw, gentle despite every instinct to grip tighter. “Neither do I. But I want to watch it happen. If you’ll let me.” He hums again, that soft, wordless tune, and this time I lean into it, letting the sound fill the spaces where my certainty used to be. In the darkroom, under the red lie of the safelight, we’re both developing—exposed, vulnerable, and suddenly, terrifyingly, beautifully alive.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's perspective as requested, maintaining her assertive personality while showing genuine growth and vulnerability. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional atmosphere that serves the story's themes of identity and self-discovery. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani feels authentic as a 25-year-old art student - confident but not infallible, attracted to Elliot's unconventional presentation while grappling with what this means for her own identity. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite limited dialogue, with his vulnerability and self-questioning rendered believably. The age gap and power dynamics are handled sensitively. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative imagery that never feels forced. The opening line "The safelight turns everything the color of old blood and secrets" immediately establishes mood and setting. Photography metaphors are woven throughout naturally, enhancing rather than overwhelming the narrative. Phrases like "eyes like undeveloped film" and "masculinity isn't a monument; it's a draft, a sketch in charcoal" demonstrate sophisticated metaphorical thinking. The emotional complexity is well-executed. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge nature and her desire to give Elliot agency creates genuine tension. Her questioning of her own identity in response to her attraction adds layers that feel earned rather than manufactured. The awkwardness of their interaction feels realistic for the situation. The dialogue, while limited, rings true. Elliot's confession about "developing in the wrong chemical bath" perfectly captures his character while maintaining the photography theme. The conversation flows naturally without feeling overly polished or unrealistic for the characters' ages and relationship. The pacing builds effectively toward the kiss, which feels earned given the emotional groundwork laid. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful but uncertain, acknowledging that both characters are in transition without forcing false resolution. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical language borders on being too clever, and some internal monologue that edges toward telling rather than showing, though these instances are relatively rare and don't significantly detract from the overall quality. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 5.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3.0, Unearned Transformations: 4.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The guidebook insisted the Trevi Fountain revealed its soul only to the solitary dawn visitor. Ben Whitmore had arrived at eight precisely, tweed jacket buttoned against the Roman heat, notebook in hand, framing Bernini’s cascade through his spectacles with the reverence of a man cataloguing grace. He was stepping backward—one careful, scholarly step—when the Vespa screamed. It did not stop so much as swerve, a black comet with crimson flames painted along its flank. The front wheel halted an inch from Ben’s knee, spraying a fine mist of fountain water across his trousers. “Madonna!” The rider killed the engine and pulled off her helmet. Black hair tumbled free, wild and untamed. Her eyes—dark espresso, sharp—took in his flustered posture with immediate, wicked amusement. “Are you feeding yourself to the angels, *inglese*? Or is this some British ritual—death by Baroque?” Ben stammered, adjusting his glasses, which had slipped. “I—goodness. I’m terribly sorry. I was—absorbed. The guidebook said—” “The guidebook can kiss my *scusa*.” She swung off the bike, all lean muscle and mischief, leather jacket creaking. “You stand in the road with your nose in dead paper. Typical. You are lost, no? Not just here. Everywhere.” “I’m Benjamin. Whitmore. I’m on sabbatical—urban Baroque planning—” “Ben.” She tasted the name, smirking. “Like a obedient dog. Say something in Italian, Ben. Say you are sorry.” “I’m… *mi dispiace molto*?” he ventured, the vowels too careful, too Oxford. She threw her head back and laughed—a sound like breaking crystal in sunlight. “Perfect. You sound like a priest at a funeral. Come. I will show you Rome before you apologize yourself into the Tiber.” He should have declined. He was a man of schedules, of footnotes, of a recently and politely ended engagement that felt like returning a library book overdue. Instead, he found himself perched behind her on the Vespa, arms uncertain around her waist, as she wove through traffic like a needle through silk, shouting at pedestrians in rapid-fire Roman dialect that he understood not a word of—except the joy in it. At a red light near the Pantheon, she reached past a street vendor’s display of postcards and slipped one into her jacket—an image of the Colosseum bleeding gold at sunset. Ben saw the motion, blinked behind his fogged glasses, and said nothing. It seemed, in her orbit, like a kind of magic trick. She took him not to monuments but to a hidden courtyard in Trastevere where laundry hung like surrender flags and an old woman sold lemons from a window. Alessandra ordered espresso with a flick of her hand, then turned to Ben with a grin that was all challenge. “You speak like a dictionary, professore. Say something wrong. Say, ‘I want chaos.’” “I want… chaos?” he tried. “Good. You are learning.” She told him she delivered packages—messages, sometimes secrets—on her bike, living in a flat with no rules and one stolen plant she refused to water. He admitted, slowly, about the sabbatical, the empty flat in Oxford, the life he had built like a careful museum exhibit. At a small bar, she ordered amaro. While Ben fumbled with euros, her hand moved—quick, graceful—pocketing a vintage matchbook from the counter, gold lettering faded: *Roma 1960*. She struck one against her boot, lit nothing, and smiled at the flame. Then she led him up a rusted ladder behind a church in Monti. The rooftop was terracotta and sky. Rome spread below them—domes, antennae, the Tiber like silver thread. “When did you last break a rule, Ben?” she asked, sitting close enough that her knee pressed his. “Never,” he admitted. “Not really.” She leaned in. “Then you are overdue.” She did not kiss him. Instead, she reached up and plucked a jasmine blossom from a vine creeping over the wall—stolen from some hidden garden—and tucked it behind her ear. Ben watched, mesmerized, realizing she collected moments the way others collected stamps: quickly, without permission, utterly without regret. As they descended near Piazza Navona, a man in a worn leather jacket called out: “Alessandra! Still borrowing the world?” She turned, wary and amused. “Luca. You still selling lies?” Luca looked Ben up and down with the knowing weariness of an old flame. “Inglese, listen. She is beautiful, yes? But she is wily. She takes things—your time, your heart, your lighter if you leave it on the table. Do not expect her to return them.” Alessandra rolled her eyes, but Ben watched her hand move—swift, unashamed—slipping Luca’s silver lighter from the stone ledge beside them. She held it up, grinning. “See? He knows me.” Ben should have felt alarm. Instead, he felt a strange, dangerous pride, as if being warned was an invitation. The sun bled orange over the Tiber by the time she drove him to the Ponte Sisto. The Vespa stopped. The air smelled of river water and possibility. “I have one more delivery,” she said. Ben nodded, his heart doing something profoundly unacademic. “Will I see you?” She did not answer. Instead, she leaned close, her breath warm against his neck, her lips brushing his ear. “You have something of mine.” Then she kissed him—quick, fierce, tasting of espresso and mischief—and was gone, the engine roaring into the Roman dusk like a question left hanging. Ben stood on the bridge, disoriented, utterly smitten. He reached into his jacket pocket for his handkerchief and found, instead, the weight of her day: the fountain coin she had lifted from the water’s edge, the matchbook, Luca’s lighter, and the jasmine—wilted now, but fragrant. She had planted her stolen treasures inside his coat, leaving him with the evidence of a life he had never planned to live. He smiled, knowing he would not return them. He would not even try. Behind him, Rome glowed, ancient and lawless, and Ben Whitmore—scholar, gentleman, careful man—realized he had been thoroughly, gloriously robbed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and vivid storytelling within the contemporary romance framework. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge through action and dialogue rather than exposition. Alessandra's kleptomaniacal tendencies are woven seamlessly into the narrative through subtle, well-observed moments—the postcard, matchbook, jasmine, and lighter—creating a character trait that feels organic rather than forced. The prose style is confident and evocative, with particularly strong imagery ("black comet with crimson flames," "laundry hung like surrender flags"). The author demonstrates skill in balancing descriptive language without falling into purple prose territory. The pacing moves effectively between intimate dialogue moments and kinetic action sequences on the Vespa. The dialogue feels authentic to each character—Ben's overly polite, academic stammering contrasts well with Alessandra's sharp, colloquial Italian-inflected English. The cultural clash is handled with nuance, avoiding stereotypes while still highlighting genuine differences in temperament and worldview. The story structure follows a clear arc through the day, building tension and intimacy through shared experiences. The introduction of Luca provides effective external validation of Alessandra's character while adding complexity to her backstory. The ending is particularly well-crafted—the revelation that she has planted her stolen items in his coat creates a satisfying reversal that feels both surprising and inevitable. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain metaphors occasionally feel slightly overwrought ("death by Baroque," "museum exhibit" for his life). The transformation of Ben from cautious academic to willing participant, while believable, happens quite rapidly. Some dialogue exchanges, particularly early on, lean toward the theatrical rather than naturalistic. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements, creating interconnected scenes that reveal character through action and establishing the foundation for a larger romantic narrative. The kleptomania element is handled with appropriate subtlety, and the cultural dynamics feel authentic rather than clichéd. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Last Outpost smells like burnt coffee and recycled oxygen with a hint of synthetic jasmine—probably whatever Kess is wearing tonight. I’m behind the bar, rag in hand, wiping the same spot near the Vega tap, round and round in small, useless circles. The wood’s already clean. It’s always clean. But my hands need the motion, especially when my ears are working overtime. Jory’s elbows are on the counter, his face long with tragedy. “She left me on read, Nets. Forty-eight hours. Two full cycles.” Kess, polishing a glass with surgical aggression, snorts. “You sent her a voice memo comparing her eyes to a nebula. That’s not romance, Jory. That’s a weather report with delusions of grandeur.” “I was being poetic!” “You were being loud.” I keep wiping. “Poetry’s subjective. Silence, though? Silence usually means no.” “Exactly,” Kess says. “She’s just busy,” Jory mutters. “She’s busy avoiding you,” Kess says. “And if you ask me to mediate one more time, I’m charging you.” “I’d pay,” Jory sighs. “You’re the only one who listens.” The bell over the door chimes—a tired, metallic sigh. I glance up. The stranger is wrong for this place. Everyone here wears grease-stained jumpsuits or loose merchant robes that have seen too many bad deals. This one wears a coat that might have been elegant once, now torn at the hem, dust from some distant moon clinging to the fabric. Their hair—tentacled dreads, dark and iridescent—hangs askew, one tentacle tangled with a broken comm-bead. They move like they’re running on fumes, eyes scanning too fast, too sharp, landing on every exit. They slide onto the stool at the far end, away from the warm lights. “Vega whisky,” they say. Their voice is hoarse, like they’ve been shouting into a storm. “Neat. Better make that a double.” I pour. The bottle’s heavy, familiar. I slide the glass over. “Rough jump?” I ask. They down half in one swallow. “Rough life. You wouldn’t understand.” “Try me. I’m good with problems.” The stranger’s eyes—amber, vertical pupils—fix on me. Too long. Then they laugh, brittle. “You’re Nettle. The bartender who fixes things.” “It’s Nets,” I say. My rag stops. “To my friends.” “Netta Voss.” My stomach drops like a broken elevator. I start wiping again, harder, the same spot, as if I can scrub the name away. “Wrong person.” “No.” They lean in, breath smelling of metal and fear. “You’re her. I have fragments of your file. The Veil doesn’t forget faces, even ones that change.” Jory and Kess have gone quiet. I feel them listening, holding their breath. “Who are you?” I ask. “Someone who made mistakes.” The stranger finishes the drink, sets the glass down with a sharp clink. “They’re docking at Ring Seven. Two ships. No transponders. I led them here, Nets. Not on purpose—they traced my signal. I needed to warn you before they walked through that door.” My hands are still. The rag is pressed flat against the bar, right over that old scar in the wood. “Why come here if you brought them?” I ask. “Because you ran once. You can run again. Or fight. But you can’t stay hidden behind this bar forever, wiping the same spot like it matters.” The docking klaxon blares—low, insistent, vibrating through the floor plates. I look at the stranger. I look at Jory and Kess, suddenly strangers in my sanctuary. “What do you want from me?” I ask. “A ship. A pilot. Or a hiding place.” They stand, pulling their torn coat tight. Their tentacled dreads tremble. “Choose fast, Netta. They’re already walking the docks.” The door chimes again. Not tired. Sharp. Decisive. Footsteps. Heavy boots on metal grating. The stranger’s eyes go wide. I haven’t turned around. The rag is still soaked in my hand, useless. “Well?” they whisper. “What kind of home is this, really? A place you hide, or a place you stand?” The boots stop just behind me. I smell ozone and something like burnt sugar—ship fuel, maybe, or worse. I keep wiping the same spot. “I don’t know yet,” I say. The stranger grabs my wrist—not hard, but urgent. “Then decide. Before they decide for you.” I finally turn. Two figures stand in the doorway. Tall. Unmarked coats. Faces hidden behind reflective visors that catch the bar’s dim light. The stranger steps back, closer to me. Jory stands up slowly. Kess sets her glass down with a click. The figures don’t move. They just watch. My rag is still in my other hand. I don’t let go. “Anyone else want a drink?” I ask, my voice steadier than I feel. “Because I think we’re about to have company.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering approximately 1000 words in first person present tense with roughly 60% dialogue as requested. The author establishes the cozy sci-fi setting effectively through sensory details like "burnt coffee and recycled oxygen with synthetic jasmine" and creates immediate conflict when the stranger arrives with dangerous pursuers. The characterization is nuanced, particularly Nets herself, who emerges as someone competent yet haunted, seeking normalcy while harboring a dangerous past. Her nervous habit of wiping the same spot reveals character through action rather than exposition. The supporting characters Jory and Kess feel authentic in their banter about failed romance, establishing the cozy atmosphere before tension arrives. The dialogue flows naturally and serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and building atmosphere. The stranger's desperate urgency contrasts well with Nets' measured responses, and the casual conversation between regulars feels genuine to the setting. The prose maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing the cozy elements with mounting tension. Descriptive passages are economical but evocative, particularly the stranger's appearance and the sensory details of the bar environment. The writing avoids purple prose while still creating vivid imagery. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from mundane bar conversation to life-threatening danger. The author wisely doesn't resolve the conflict, leaving readers with a cliffhanger as requested. The ending maintains appropriate darkness given the circumstances without forced positivity. Some minor weaknesses include occasional reliance on familiar sci-fi tropes (mysterious past, faceless pursuers) and a few moments where the tension could be tighter. However, the execution elevates these elements through strong character work and atmospheric writing. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 86.8
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The trucker’s parting words are “Watch yourself, kid,” which is hilarious, because nobody’s ever watched out for me—not even me. I slide out of the cab and the door slams. The air in New Orleans isn’t air; it’s soup. It’s August, or maybe hell’s Tuesday, and I’m standing on some street called Canal with a backpack that weighs maybe twelve pounds but feels like it’s full of bricks. The strap slips. Again. It always slips. I hoist it back onto my shoulder, the nylon burning where it’s frayed, and I think: *This is freedom. This is what running away feels like. It feels like sweat in places you didn’t know could sweat.* Two days ago, I was in our kitchen in Millbrook, Tennessee, population “who cares,” main export “disappointment.” In a town like that, your choices are a) be mind-fuckingly bored, or b) invent some drama. I’d done both. I’d cut classes. I’d fought with teachers. I’d stayed out until the streetlights came on just to feel the dark. But then there was the school counsellor—stupid, smug, calling Mom on the phone with that voice like she was diagnosing a broken appliance. “Concerning behavioural patterns,” she said. Like I’m a spreadsheet with errors. Like coming out isn’t the least concerning thing I’ve ever done. But it wasn’t invented drama. This was the real deal, and a long time coming. I told them. Mom had her hands on the counter like she was holding herself up. Dad sat at the table, not looking at me. I said, “I’m trans. I’m not your daughter. I’m—” and then I couldn’t finish because Mom made this sound, like a tire hitting a pothole, and Dad said, “We need to talk about this calmly,” which is dad-code for *you’re seeking attention again*. The last forty-eight hours had been pure disaster. Screaming. The Bible quoted like a weapon. Mom crying behind the locked bathroom door. Dad telling me I was “confused.” So I hatched it in thirty minutes—backpack, cash from the jar in the garage, forty-three dollars and some change, a bus to Memphis, then a trucker who smelled like Marlboros and regret, then another ride, then “Get out, I don’t do runaways.” That was last night. Now I’m two states away, unceremoniously ejected in downtown New Orleans, and the strap slips a third time. I catch it with my elbow and keep walking. I need water. I need a bathroom. I need to not be sixteen and alone in a city that smells like fried dough and rot. The French Quarter is loud—jazz spilling out of doorways, tourists with cameras, people who belong. I don’t belong anywhere. That’s the point, I guess. I keep my head down, my hair—short, dyed black in a gas station bathroom two months ago—falling over my eyes. I’m wearing a hoodie even though it’s ninety degrees, because layers hide things. The binder is tight under there, and I’m breathing shallow, but it’s the only thing that makes my chest feel right. The only real thing I have left. By noon I’m sitting on a bench near an iron-fenced park—Jackson Square, maybe—watching a street performer paint himself silver. My feet hurt. The strap has slipped four times in ten minutes. I’m trying to tie it with a hair tie I found in my pocket, but the knot keeps coming loose. “You’re gonna lose that bag if you keep fighting it like that.” The voice is close. I jump. A kid—maybe seventeen, maybe older—stands there with hands in the pockets of cargo pants that have seen better decades. They have brown skin, hair in braids with beads that click when they move, and eyes that look like they’ve already seen every trick I’m about to try. “I’m fine,” I say. My voice cracks. I hate that. “You’re not. You’re also bleeding.” I look down. There’s a scrape on my palm I don’t remember getting. Maybe from the truck. Maybe from the world. The kid—Ren, they say later—sits on the bench without asking. They smell like rain and something sharp, like electricity before a storm. “I’m Ren,” they say. “Taylor.” “Cool. You running?” The question is direct, like a needle. I don’t answer. Instead: “You a cop?” Ren laughs. It’s not nice; it’s tired. “Do I look like a cop?” “No.” “Good. I’m running too. Or I was. Now I’m just... here.” They tilt their head. “You got a place?” “I just got here.” “Yesterday? Today?” “Last night. Got kicked out of a ride downtown.” Ren nods like this is normal. Maybe for them it is. “You need food. You look like a ghost that lost its sheet.” “I have money.” “How much?” I don’t answer. The strap slips again, hanging loose like a broken promise. Ren reaches out and adjusts it—just pulls it up, tightens the buckle with practiced fingers. “You need a carabiner,” they say. “Or a new bag. But mostly you need to not sit in the open like a lost puppy. This city eats lost puppies.” There’s something in the way they say it—not threatening, but not gentle either. A warning wrapped in observation. We talk. Or Ren talks, and I listen, suspicious. They tell me about the house—an old shotgun place in the Marigny, half-collapsed, shared by kids who don’t ask last names. They tell me they’re seventeen, from Baton Rouge, running from a stepfather who “had issues with boundaries.” They don’t say more. Secrets hang off them like the beads in their hair—heavy, noisy, unavoidable. But there are other secrets too. I notice the way Ren checks over their shoulder. The expensive-looking watch on their wrist, scratched and dirty, but still expensive. The fact that they have two phones in their pocket, one cracked, one not. The way they ask me questions—where I’m from, what I’m running from—but never answer mine with anything solid. “I’m not going to hurt you,” Ren says, reading my face. “But I’m not going to save you either. You want to come crash? Come. You want to keep wandering until some tourist offers you ‘help’ that costs more than you have? Your choice.” The conflict is physical—my stomach knots, my throat tightens. I want to trust someone. I want to be alone. I want to go home and I want to burn the home down. I want Mom to understand and I want to never see her face again. “I’m trans,” I blurt out, like a test. “That’s why I left. They—my family—” Ren just nods. “Cool. I’m whatever works today. You need a binder? I got extras. You need to talk? I don’t do therapy. But I do food.” The strap slips one more time. Ren fixes it again, tying it with one of their hair beads and a knot that looks like it might actually hold. For a second, the touch is kind. Too kind. I wonder what it costs. We walk. The city unfolds—colorful houses, music, the smell of gumbo and garbage. Ren leads me through streets that feel like they’re breathing. I think about the counsellor, about “concerning behavioural patterns,” about how running away is the most concerning thing I’ve ever done, and also the most honest. Inside, I’m a war. Relief fights terror. Freedom fights loneliness. The gender I finally named fighting the body I was born into, fighting the family that won’t see it, fighting the world that might kill me for it. Ren stops at a corner store. They buy me water, a sandwich, and don’t ask for money back. They tell me about the dangers—cops who don’t care, men who do care too much, the heat that kills, the drugs that seem like friends. “You’re smart,” Ren says, biting into something they bought for themself. “But smart doesn’t keep you safe. Smart just means you see the knife coming.” “Do you have one?” I ask. “A knife?” Ren smiles, but it doesn’t reach their eyes. “I have a lot of things, Taylor. Come on. The house isn’t far. You can decide if I’m a danger or just another runaway with issues.” The strap is finally secure, tied with Ren’s bead and a knot that holds. I follow. The first day isn’t over. The danger isn’t defined. But for the first time in forty-eight hours, I’m not running alone. And I don’t know if that’s salvation or just a different kind of disaster.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's voice as a transgender runaway navigating their first day in New Orleans. The writing maintains a consistent first-person perspective with effective mixed tense usage, and the 1000-word count is well-utilized. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor emerges as a believable, complex teenager - angry, vulnerable, defiant, and desperately seeking connection while maintaining necessary wariness. Their internal monologue feels authentic, capturing the conflicted emotions of someone fleeing family rejection while facing an uncertain future. The recurring motif of the slipping backpack strap serves as an effective metaphor for Taylor's precarious situation and need for stability. Ren is equally well-crafted as a character with clear depth and mystery. Their dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, revealing personality through speech patterns and word choices. The hints at their own secrets (expensive watch, two phones, practiced knowledge of survival) create intrigue without feeling forced. The prose quality is generally high, with vivid sensory details that bring New Orleans to life - the "soup" air, the smells of "fried dough and rot," the sounds of jazz spilling from doorways. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining descriptive richness. Metaphors like "secrets hang off them like the beads in their hair" are effective without being overwrought. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Taylor's internal war between relief and terror, freedom and loneliness. The piece doesn't shy away from the real dangers facing a transgender runaway while also showing moments of unexpected kindness. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases feel slightly forced ("hell's Tuesday," "population 'who cares'"), and some of the internal monologue occasionally borders on being too articulate for a distressed teenager, though this is minimal. The pacing is generally strong, though there are brief moments where the narrative slows slightly during exposition. The dialogue is naturalistic and serves character development well. Ren's speech patterns effectively convey their street-smart experience while Taylor's responses show their vulnerability and wariness. The conversation flows organically and reveals character rather than simply conveying information. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither falsely optimistic nor gratuitously dark. Taylor's situation remains precarious, but there's a realistic glimmer of hope in finding someone who understands, even if that person may bring their own complications. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Bunker of Reasonable Expectations was not, strictly speaking, expecting anything reasonable that Tuesday. The air smelled of rust, regret, and the particular melancholy of tinned beans that had long since given up on being food. Midas sat on a crate stenciled with the words *Caution: Contains Hope*—a warning he treated with the reverence of a man who had once opened a can of hope and found only despair inside, neatly vacuum-packed. He was polishing his knife with the enthusiasm of a man performing a tax audit on his own soul. From a rusted pipe above hung Chewy: an animatronic stuffed koala, salvaged from some long-forgotten nursery display of the Before Time. His left eye flickered with the dim, apologetic amber of a streetlamp that had seen too much. His right eye had long since surrendered to entropy. His stitching had given up on dignity years ago, leaving his left paw dangling by a thread of synthetic fur and stubborn optimism. “We need to go out,” Midas said. “Go out and play!” Chewy chirped, his voice box crackling like a campfire made of legal disclaimers. “Parental supervision not included!” “Not play. The Mall of Perpetual Commerce. Electronics Graveyard, sector seven. There’s a power cell there—nine volts, maybe twelve, my engineering is more prayer than science. If we don’t replace your battery, you’ll loop slogans until the rats organize a union and demand hazard pay.” Chewy’s ear twitched, emitting a static wheeze that might have been agreement or indigestion. “Just Do It!” he announced brightly. “Results not guaranteed. Side effects may include enlightenment, nausea, or sudden awareness of mortality.” Midas sighed. It was the kind of sigh that had settled into his bones like a tenant who refused to pay rent. “The plan is simple. We enter through the service entrance. We avoid the Pigeon Bureaucrats—they’ve been demanding forms in triplicate since the Collapse. We avoid the Sentient Shopping Carts, which have developed both consciousness and a grudge. We retrieve the cell. We return. No heroics. No discoveries. Just procurement.” “Because you’re worth it!” Chewy said, swaying gently. “Excluding tax, dignity, and applicable jurisdictions.” “And keep your slogans to yourself,” Midas added, pulling on a coat that had been three different colors in three different lives. “Last night you told the mold colony they were ‘lovin’ it,’ and now they’ve formed a choir.” *** The Wastes were as welcoming as a root canal performed by a blind dentist with a grudge. The sky wore the color of old bruises. The wind carried whispers of dead advertisements, ghostly jingles that made the hair on Midas’s neck stand up in solidarity with his common sense. They walked. The Mall of Perpetual Commerce loomed like a cathedral built by people who had confused shopping with spirituality. Its doors stood open, which in the Wastes was never an invitation but a warning written in large, negligent letters. Inside, the air smelled of ozone, expired perfume, and the particular sorrow of things that had once been important. A cult of survivors had established itself in the Food Court, worshipping a vending machine named Kevin. Kevin dispensed energy drinks that had expired before the Collapse, which made them, in the cult’s theology, sacred relics of a purer age. The leader approached: a thin man in a tie woven from plastic shopping bags, carrying a clipboard like a scepter. “You seek the Electronics Graveyard,” he said. It was not a question. “We seek a battery,” Midas said. “Small. Cylindrical. Capable of preventing auditory terrorism.” “Kevin demands tribute.” Midas handed over a button. A large, yellow button that said *Press for Service*. The leader examined it with the gravity of an archaeologist unearthing a king’s tomb. “Kevin accepts. But you must wear the seal.” He affixed a sticky circle to Midas’s chest: *Customer Satisfaction: Pending*. “Proceed,” the leader said. “But know that Kevin sees all. Even in death, especially in death, he watches.” They proceeded. The Food Court had become a pit of fermented yogurt—vast, bubbling, and philosophically ambitious. It had achieved a kind of sentience, though its thoughts were mostly about texture and belonging. Midas, being Midas, stepped in precisely the wrong place. As he sank to his knees in a substance that smelled of vanilla and broken promises, Chewy emitted a slogan with the force of destiny: “Taste the rainbow of tomorrow!” The yogurt shuddered. The words, it seemed, were a command from its ancient marketing masters, embedded deep in its molecular memory. It parted with a sound like a disappointed sigh, allowing Midas to crawl out coated in slime that glowed faintly in the dark. “Thank you,” Midas said, wiping his eyes. “You’re welcome,” Chewy said. “Service charges apply to all rescue operations, emotional or physical.” *** The Electronics Graveyard was a cathedral of dead screens, silent telephones, and the hushed reverence of technology that had outlived its purpose. They found the cell in a display case marked *Premium Power Solutions—Now 100% Obsolete*. Midas reached for it. The case hummed. Lights—ancient, tired lights—flickered on. A hologram materialized: a woman named Glenda, translucent, smiling with teeth too white for a dead world. “Welcome back, valued customer!” she said, her voice the auditory equivalent of a handshake that lasted too long. “How may I optimize your apocalypse experience today?” “We just want the battery,” Midas said. “Excellent choice! Before I can assist, please rate your satisfaction with our end-of-world services on a scale of one to five, where five is ‘ecstatic transcendence’ and one is ‘existential dread with extra dread.’” “We’re in a hurry,” Midas said. Glenda’s smile did not waver. “There’s no rush! Time is a social construct, and so is this survey. Your feedback helps us serve you better in this life and the next, assuming there is a next, which our legal department neither confirms nor denies.” Midas grabbed the battery. Alarms—tired, bureaucratic alarms—began to wail. The escape was not so much a flight as a negotiation with chaos. Behind them, the Sanitation Protocol activated. Giant automated cleaners—industrial discs with brushes and an almost religious thirst for cleanliness—rolled out from the walls, humming with purpose. “Sweepy! Mop! Engage hygiene!” Midas and Chewy ran. Through Sporting Goods, past the Fountain of Eternal Discount, into the Service Tunnels where the air tasted of copper and old secrets. Here, as fate intended, they were separated. Chewy was snagged by a malfunctioning escalator that suddenly decided to ascend at lethal velocity. He was flung upward—*whoosh*—through a skylight, onto the roof. From there he slid down a collapsed ventilation shaft, a journey that stripped him of what little dignity remained, and landed with a *plop* precisely in the Bunker’s mail slot. He tumbled into the main room, covered in dust, clutching the battery, and hanging by his stitching from the pipe once more. Midas, meanwhile, was chased into an elevator shaft. The doors—miraculously, maliciously—closed, then opened onto a garbage compactor. He was pressed, not fatally but thoroughly, into a neat rectangular cube of refuse. The cube was loaded onto a scavenger cart pulled by mutated rats in tiny harnesses. The rats, recognizing the scent of fermented yogurt, panicked. The cart careened through the Wastes, back toward the Bunker, and crashed through the side door with the enthusiasm of a comet that had forgotten its manners. Midas—now rectangular, yogurt-coated, and missing an eyebrow—tumbled into the storage room. But there was a third return. Mop, the cleaning drone, had developed an attachment. It had followed them through the ventilation, rolling with the determination of a philosophy that refuses to be ignored. It entered the kitchen and began polishing Midas’s last clean spoon with a tenderness that bordered on the inappropriate. So they came back in multiple ways: Chewy via skylight, shaft, and postal system; Midas via compression, rodent logistics, and structural damage; and Mop via loyalty, ventilation, and a complete disregard for personal boundaries. All were worse for wear. The Bunker now contained a rectangular man, a dangling koala, a drone in love with silverware, and a yellow sticker that read *Pending*. Midas crawled out of the rubble. He looked at Chewy. He looked at Mop. He looked at the battery in Chewy’s paw—except it was not the right battery. It was a toothbrush battery, 1.5 volts, utterly useless, and probably sentient in ways best not investigated. “Have a nice day!” Chewy said, his eye flickering. “Results may vary. Warranty void where prohibited by law or apocalypse.” Midas peeled the sticker from his chest. He looked at the drone, which was now polishing his knife. “Tomorrow,” he said, his voice flat with the wisdom of a man who had learned nothing and expected less, “we stay in.” Outside, the Wastes continued. Inside, the Bunker smelled of yogurt, defeat, and the faint, lingering echo of a slogan no one had ever truly understood. But they were home. In pieces, in slime, in multiple ways—but home.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, absurdist humor, and philosophical undertones. The author effectively employs Pratchett's characteristic technique of treating the absurd with deadpan seriousness while finding profound meaning in mundane details. The world-building is particularly strong, creating a post-apocalyptic setting that feels both familiar and surreal. The "Mall of Perpetual Commerce" and "Bunker of Reasonable Expectations" are perfectly Pratchettesque locations that serve both comedic and thematic purposes. The author demonstrates excellent understanding of how consumer culture might persist in bizarre forms after societal collapse. Character development is well-executed within the constraints of the format. Midas emerges as a believably weary survivor whose competence is consistently undermined by circumstances beyond his control. Chewy, despite being an animatronic koala, becomes oddly endearing through his malfunctioning corporate slogans that occasionally prove unexpectedly useful. The relationship between them feels genuine and lived-in. The prose quality is consistently high, with memorable phrases like "polishing his knife with the enthusiasm of a man performing a tax audit on his own soul" that perfectly capture Pratchett's voice. The author maintains the delicate balance between humor and melancholy that defines Pratchett's best work. The plot structure adheres well to the prompt's requirements, following the mission from planning through inevitable complications to multiple returns home. The escalating absurdity feels organic rather than forced, and the various mishaps serve both comedic and character development purposes. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Occasionally the Pratchett pastiche becomes slightly heavy-handed, with some metaphors pushed just beyond their natural limits. The ending, while appropriately downbeat, could have used slightly more emotional resonance to fully capitalize on the relationship between the characters. The dialogue, primarily Chewy's corporate slogans, serves its purpose well but is somewhat limited by the character's nature. The piece maintains strong coherence throughout and avoids common pitfalls like overwrought prose or unearned emotional beats. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
