+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 89.3
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** Incident Log 7392-Theta / Associated with SCP-7392 **Site:** 19 **Reporting Researcher:** Dr. Elias Vance, Containment Psychology, Level 3 **Date of Filing:** October 15th, 2024 **Status:** CLASSIFIED — COGNITOHAZARD / TEMPORAL REVIEW PENDING I wrote this account in past tense because, by the time I finished it, I was no longer certain that present tense applied to me consistently. The Foundation requested coherence. I tried to provide it. I had been assigned to SCP-7392—designated “The Mnemosyne Construct”—a dormant Class-IV psychic entity secured in Sub-Level 4. My duties involved behavioral monitoring and archival correlation, not temporal mechanics. I had no reason to expect correspondence from myself. The first note appeared on October 2nd. I found it inside my locked desk drawer, beneath a stack of blank Foundation memo paper I kept for drafting reports. The drawer required a biometric scan and a four-digit code I had not shared with anyone. Security footage from the previous twelve hours showed no entry, no motion, no anomaly except the paper itself. It was standard stock: white, blue header lines, “SITE-19 / CLASSIFIED” watermark, identical to the reams in my supply closet. The message was brief, rendered in my handwriting, though the letters trembled more violently than mine ever had. > **NOTE 1 (reproduced):** > “Don’t open Vault B on the 14th. The breach isn’t an escape—it’s a replacement. You won’t notice until you’ve already filed the false report. Burn this. —E” I should have burned it. Instead, I filed an incident report. The Temporal Anomalies Division was consulted. They detected no chronometric distortion in my office, no localized time dilation, no causal loop signatures. Handwriting analysis returned a 98.7% match to my own, with stress indicators consistent with extreme duress—far beyond my baseline. Meanwhile, SCP-7392 remained dormant. Its vitals were flat. Its chamber was untouched. But the notes continued. > **NOTE 2 (recovered October 5th, found inside my sealed briefcase, which I had not opened since locking it the prior evening):** > “The flicker happens at 03:14. You think it’s a drill. The lights don’t come back on in the east wing. I have already told you this three times but you keep forgetting. The entity doesn’t want out. It wants the paperwork correct. Check the clock in Observation Room 4. It runs backward when you’re alone. Don’t be alone.” This was the first contradiction. The clock in Observation Room 4 was analog, battery-operated, and had run forward without fault since its installation in 2018. I checked it that same afternoon, alone. It ticked forward. I felt a nagging wrongness about the architecture of Site-19 itself—a dissonance I could not place, as though the walls were arranged differently in my memory than in my eyes. I dismissed it as fatigue. The Foundation expanded the investigation. Dr. Aris Thorne from Cognitohazard Containment proposed psychological manipulation—either SCP-7392 projecting false episodic memories into my subconscious, or a secondary memetic agent using my own handwriting patterns as a vector. I was subjected to Class-W mnestic evaluation. I passed, technically, though the psychologist noted “unusual resistance patterns in episodic recall” and a “refusal to integrate contradictory data.” I began locking away all memo paper. I kept my supplies in a safe in my quarters, combination changed daily. I wrote exclusively on digital tablets, my fingers never touching ink. It made no difference. > **NOTE 3 (October 8th, discovered tucked inside my pillowcase. The safe in my quarters remained unopened; security logs confirmed no access):** > “YOU LOCKED THE PAPER AWAY BUT THE PAPER IS THE DOOR. I AM WRITING THIS FROM THE WALLS. THE BREACH IS NOT ON THE 14TH ANYMORE. IT MOVED. YOU MOVED IT BY READING. SITE-19 IS NOT UNDERGROUND. YOU KNOW THIS IS A LIE. TRUST THE LIE. THE LIE IS SAFER. —E (NOT YOU)” The contradictions escalated beyond simple error. Site-19 was, of course, underground—Sub-Level 4 existed in verifiable physical reality, confirmed by structural surveys and geological reports. Yet the note insisted I “knew” this was false, and in the moment of reading, I did feel a phantom certainty that the earth above us was an illusion, that we floated in some other geometry. The clock, the architecture, the year—all of it felt negotiable. The Foundation ordered full psychological isolation. I was moved to monitored quarters in Sector 7, away from SCP-7392’s containment range by three security bulkheads. Cameras watched me sleep. I was permitted no writing instruments except during supervised interviews, conducted through glass. On October 11th, I woke with ink stains on my fingertips. > **NOTE 4 (found clipped to my medical chart, written on standard memo paper that did not match any supply in Sector 7. The paper bore my fingerprints and traces of my blood type, O-positive):** > “They are testing if I’m real. You are the test. The containment breach already happened in 2019 and we covered it up with this report. Read the date on your clearance badge. It says 2024. That year doesn’t exist here. The entity is not 7392. 7392 is the story we tell so you don’t look at the paper. Don’t look at the paper. I am screaming inside the blue lines.” My clearance badge read 2024. The year was correct. But seeing it declared false made my vision narrow. The Foundation’s Temporal Division ran a full-body chronometric scan. They found “micro-fluctuations”—not sufficient to prove temporal displacement, but enough to rule out pure psychological fabrication. Or so they claimed. Dr. Thorne argued the opposite. He presented data showing SCP-7392’s psychic signature had spiked on October 11th—briefly, for 0.4 seconds—corresponding exactly with the appearance of Note 4. “It’s feeding on your anxiety,” he told me through the glass. “Creating a feedback loop. The notes are its voice, using your hand, your paper, your fear.” But if it was SCP-7392, why did the notes warn me about SCP-7392? Note 5 addressed this with a logic that unraveled as I read it. > **NOTE 5 (October 12th, found inside my mouth—literally folded behind my molars, soaked in saliva, though the paper itself was dry except for my own moisture):** > “If it is 7392 then I am already dead and writing this from digestion. The breach is the report itself. Every word you file makes the door wider. The 14th is a lie I told to keep you reading. There is no date. There is only the next page. Lock me out. You can’t. I’m the one holding the pen.” This was the breaking point. The incoherence was total. The contradictions were not merely factual—Site-19’s location, the clock, the year—but ontological. The notes denied their own existence, then demanded action based on that denial. I could no longer determine whether I was the victim of a temporal anomaly, the puppet of a contained entity, or the author of my own dissolution. I requested termination of the study. I requested amnestic therapy. The Foundation denied both, citing the need to understand the vector. Then came the final note. October 13th. The day before the predicted breach. > **NOTE 6 (found burned at the edges, pinned to my chest with a paperclip folded from the same memo stock, the sheet creased into an impossible geometry that did not match standard folding patterns):** > “THE VAULT IS OPEN YOU OPENED IT WITH YOUR EYES THE PAPER IS BLEEDING BLUE LINES INTO THE WALLS I CAN SEE YOU WRITING THIS FROM THE OTHER SIDE OF THE MIRROR DON’T TRUST THE FOUNDATION THEY ARE ALREADY NOTES DON’T TRUST YOUR HANDS THEY WROTE THE BREACH REPORT BEFORE YOU WERE BORN BURN THIS BURN ME BURN THE —” The remainder was illegible, smudged with blood that the lab confirmed was mine—type O, matching—though I had no wounds upon examination. October 14th arrived. I was in Observation Room 4, alone, watching SCP-7392’s chamber through reinforced glass. At 03:14, the lights flickered. Not a drill. A failure in Sub-Level 4’s eastern grid. For three minutes, the east wing went dark. I heard nothing. I saw nothing. The clock—forward, always forward—ticked loudly in the silence. Then the lights returned. SCP-7392 was still in its chamber. Its vitals were unchanged. No physical breach had occurred. But I was holding a pen. I was writing. The report in front of me—this report—was already filled with my handwriting, describing events that had not happened yet, warning about doors I had not opened. The memo paper was standard Foundation stock. I had none with me. The Foundation is still investigating. They have isolated me. They have taken the notes—six of them, all accounted for, all physically present in the evidence locker, all written on paper that should not have existed in the spaces where they were found. Dr. Thorne believes I am a vector for SCP-7392’s projection. The Temporal Division believes I am experiencing a localized, self-generating causality loop. I believe neither. I believe I wrote them. I believe I will write them again. The breach has not happened. Or it has not happened yet. Or it is this report, and every time it is read, the vault opens a little wider. I locked away my paper. I changed the combination. I stopped writing. But my hands hurt. And the blue lines on the page are very, very wide. —Dr. Elias Vance Site-19 October 15th, 2024 **Addendum 7392-Theta-A:** Dr. Vance was found deceased in his quarters on October 16th. Cause of death: exsanguination via self-inflicted wounds consistent with writing instruments. His quarters contained no paper. His desk drawer was locked. Inside, investigators found a seventh note, written in blood on standard Foundation memo paper. > **NOTE 7:** > “He stopped writing. Good. Now you can start.” The file remains open. The vault remains closed. The paper supply in Sub-Level 4 has been requisitioned for incineration. It has not been destroyed. The blue lines remain.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the Foundation universe while executing a complex narrative structure that blends official documentation with psychological horror. The piece successfully maintains the clinical, bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP reports while gradually introducing elements of unreliability and temporal confusion. The format adherence is excellent - the piece reads authentically as a Foundation document, complete with proper classification headers, item numbers, and addenda. The author skillfully uses the constraints of the SCP format to enhance rather than limit the storytelling, with the rigid structure creating an effective contrast to the increasingly chaotic content of the notes. The characterization of Dr. Vance is nuanced and believable. His progression from skeptical researcher to confused victim feels organic, with his actions consistently matching someone experiencing either genuine temporal anomalies or sophisticated psychological manipulation. The voice remains consistent throughout, maintaining the professional detachment expected of a Foundation researcher even as the situation deteriorates. The escalating notes create genuine tension and unease. Each note becomes more desperate and contradictory, effectively building the central mystery of whether this represents temporal displacement, entity manipulation, or psychological breakdown. The contradictions serve the narrative purpose of undermining both the character's and reader's certainty about reality. The prose is elegant and controlled, avoiding purple prose while maintaining atmospheric tension. The clinical language creates an effective juxtaposition with the horror elements. The imagery is vivid without being overwrought - details like finding paper "inside my mouth" and the "blue lines bleeding into the walls" are disturbing without being gratuitously graphic. The piece demonstrates strong creativity in its approach to temporal paradox and reality distortion. The concept of the breach being the report itself is genuinely innovative, and the recursive nature of the notes creates a compelling puzzle. The ending, with Dr. Vance's death and the final note addressing the reader directly, provides a satisfying if dark conclusion that maintains the ambiguity central to the piece's effectiveness. The emotional complexity is well-handled, showing Vance's growing desperation and confusion without melodrama. The horror emerges from uncertainty and loss of agency rather than shock value. The piece earns its darkness through careful buildup and logical progression of events. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the exposition feels slightly forced, particularly in explaining Foundation procedures. The middle section occasionally risks becoming repetitive with the note discoveries, though this serves the thematic purpose of showing the escalating pattern. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-7491 **Object Class:** Keter **Special Containment Procedures:** Due to the spontaneous, non-predictable manifestation of SCP-7491-A within derelict structures worldwide, containment prioritizes suppression, access denial, and information control. Foundation webcrawlers and urban exploration monitors flag reports of “doors that do not belong” inside abandoned factories, asylums, libraries, and residential complexes. Mobile Task Force Mu-13 (“Ghost Architects”) secures affected sites; if feasible, the host structure is acquired and demolished, though demolition does not prevent re-manifestation within a 5 km radius within 72 hours. All catalogued instances of SCP-7491-A—currently 847—are physically identical regardless of host architecture. Each measures 2.1 m by 0.9 m, constructed of dark oak or rusted steel, fitted with an unmarked brass doorknob and lacking any signage, numbering, or manufacturer stamps. Every instance bears the same damage: three parallel scratches, approximately 3.8 cm in length, carved into the frame immediately surrounding the knob plate in a triangular configuration. The brass exhibits uniform oxidation patterns irrespective of local climate. Attempts to remove, destroy, or relocate an instance result in its intact reappearance within 24 hours, often in an adjacent abandoned building. Personnel entering SCP-7491-B must wear analog temporal-stabilization harnesses and record exclusively on film or paper; digital storage corrupts within 90 seconds of exposure. Amnestic protocols apply to unauthorized civilians. Under no circumstances are O5 Council members permitted entry; this directive is currently under internal review (see Addendum 7491-Omega). **Description:** SCP-7491-A instances serve as thresholds to SCP-7491-B, an interior space designated “The Archive.” From the exterior, the host buildings are in various states of decay—collapsed roofs, flooded basements, overgrown interiors. Upon opening an SCP-7491-A instance, the interior is replaced entirely by SCP-7491-B: an architecturally impossible expanse of shelving and corridors extending horizontally and vertically beyond geometric limits. The space is illuminated by a sourceless amber glow, maintains a constant 18°C, and smells of ozone, iron, and decaying paper. The floor is polished black marble regardless of the building’s original foundation. The Archive contains millions of documents—bound ledgers, loose files, photographic plates, and data storage media of unknown manufacture—organized according to an indecipherable taxonomy. Linguistic analysis has identified texts in over 400 languages, including 73 with no known terrestrial origin. The predominant subject is a global civilization referred to variously as “The First Containment,” “The Predecessor Epoch,” and “The World That Held.” These records describe an advanced society that maintained planetary stability through large-scale containment of anomalous phenomena. Their collapse is consistently attributed to a singular, unnamed event: “The Uncontained Cascade,” “The Breach of All Doors,” or “The Failure That Was Not Named.” Specific causes are never elaborated; relevant passages are either physically burned into the pages or redacted by an unknown chemical process that leaves the paper intact but the text illegible. Of critical concern are files referencing an organization structurally and functionally identical to the SCP Foundation, designated in the texts as “The Containment Directorate” (TCD), “The Bureau of Secure Phenomena,” and “The Foundation for the Preservation of Normalcy—Original Charter.” Personnel files contain biometric data, psychological profiles, and service records matching current Foundation staff. Dr. Elias Vance (Site-19, Research Lead) appears in a 1789 induction ledger as “Archivist-Prime Vance, status: eternal service.” Dr. Sarah Chen (Site-03) is listed in an 1844 payroll record. Photographs show current personnel in period clothing, standing within sections of the Archive that have since been emptied. **Addendum 7491-1: Manifestation Patterns** SCP-7491-A instances appear exclusively in buildings that have been abandoned for a minimum of five years and have experienced no human entry for at least six months. The doors manifest fully formed, often replacing existing doors or appearing in blank walls. The scratch pattern around the doorknob is always present from the moment of manifestation and never changes, even as the surrounding wood or metal weathers differently. **Addendum 7491-2: Exploration Log 7491-Theta** *The following account is reconstructed from analog audio recordings, handwritten field notes, and developed film recovered from Team Theta-7: Dr. Elena Vasquez (Lead), Agent Marcus Chen (Security), and Researcher Samuel Okonkwo (Archival Analysis). The team entered through an instance discovered in an abandoned textile mill in Manchester, United Kingdom.* Dr. Vasquez adjusted her temporal harness as the team stepped across the threshold. The factory floor—broken concrete, rusted machinery, a collapsed roof open to gray sky—vanished. In its place stretched the amber-lit canyon of SCP-7491-B. Shelving rose like black cliffs on either side, constructed from wood and brass that seemed to absorb the light rather than reflect it. There was no ceiling visible, only an upward darkness that swallowed the beam of Chen’s flashlight. “We’re on marble,” Okonkwo said, his voice flat, lacking echo. “Polished black marble. The mill didn’t have marble.” They proceeded for forty minutes by Vasquez’s analog watch—though the device later indicated only twelve minutes had elapsed—following a narrow aisle marked with brass plaques bearing an unknown script. The documents they passed were bound in leather that felt faintly warm, some emitting a rhythmic ticking like a distant clock. Then Chen stopped. “Movement. Third aisle left. Three figures.” The team turned. In the distance, three entities in white hazmat suits—unbranded, featureless black visors, gloves of an unknown matte polymer—were removing file crates from a lower shelf. They loaded the crates onto a cart of polished bone and steel with mechanical, silent efficiency. One figure paused, its head tilting at an angle that suggested observation despite the absence of visible eyes. Vasquez later noted in her journal that she felt “recognized—not as a stranger, but as a file being recalled.” Chen raised his flashlight. “Identify yourselves. Foundation personnel?” The figures did not respond. They turned in unison. The crates were abandoned where they stood. They moved with impossible speed—not running, but folding the distance between rows of shelving—vanishing into a corridor that, by all geometry, should have terminated in a solid wall of brick. The team approached the dropped crate. Inside were files labeled in English: *Containment Failure Reports—Epoch Termination*. One document had been partially removed; its remaining edges were charred black, as though burned from within. Vasquez photographed the scene with a film camera. The developed images showed the figures in the blurred background, but unmistakably present. Their suits bore a faded circular seal—three arrows arranged in a ring—identical to the Foundation’s emblem, but reversed, and crowned with a symbol resembling the scratch pattern around every SCP-7491-A doorknob. **Addendum 7491-3: Recovered Document Excerpts** The following excerpts were translated from materials recovered during Log Theta and subsequent authorized entries. > **Excerpt 7491-A** (Recovered from dropped crate, charred fragment): > “The First World did not fall to war, plague, or celestial event. We contained the sky, the sea, the thoughts of kings, and still we failed. There was a door we did not lock. There was a scratch upon its frame—three lines, a warning we ignored. When the Uncontained came, it did not destroy us. It archived us. We are not dead. We are filed.” > **Excerpt 7491-B** (Personnel File, intact, recovered from Sector 4): > “NAME: Vasquez, Elena Maria. > DESIGNATION: Lead Archivist, Sector 9. > DATE OF INDUCTION: 14 June, 1621. > CLEARANCE: Omega-Black. > NOTE: Subject demonstrates exceptional resistance to memetic dissolution. Subject has entered The Archive on seventeen documented occasions without authorization. Subject is reminded that the Archive is not a repository for the living. Status: PENDING REASSIGNMENT TO PERMANENT RECORD.” > **Excerpt 7491-C** (Administrative Memorandum, recovered from restricted sector): > “To the Council of Five: Your presence in Sector 7 is noted. You are not listed in the current ledger, yet your signatures appear in the visitor log of 1901, 1953, and 1987. The Archive remembers every entry. You may take your files, but you cannot take your history. The Curator awaits your explanation. Do not return without the Key.” **Addendum 7491-Omega: Evidence of Unauthorized O5 Council Intrusion** Embedded within the false bottom of the crate recovered in Log Theta was a leather-bound visitor log. Material analysis dates the vellum and iron-gall ink to approximately 1700 CE. However, the entries include notations in modern ballpoint ink, laser-printed annotations, and references to Foundation Site designations established in the 20th century. Notable entries include: - **“O5-1. Consultation regarding temporal displacement protocols. 14 October, 1987. Authorized: None.”** - **“O5-3, O5-7. Retrieval of personal files and biometric samples. 22 March, 2003. Authorized: Self.”** - **“O5-9. Unknown purpose. Entry duration: 47 minutes. Exit: Unrecorded.”** Handwriting analysis confirms a 98.7% match to current O5 Council members. Foundation security logs show no corresponding absences during these dates. When questioned, all O5 members denied knowledge of SCP-7491. However, prior to confiscation by unknown parties, O5-1 was observed in possession of a brass key bearing the identical three-line scratch pattern found around every SCP-7491-A doorknob. Further exploration indicates that entire sections of SCP-7491-B—sometimes spanning hundreds of meters—are being systematically emptied. The entities designated SCP-7491-C appear with increasing frequency, always fleeing upon direct observation, always leaving behind fragments referencing “The Next Containment” and “The Foundation That Must Not Remember.” All doors share the scratches. All doors open. The Archive is being edited, and the editors wear suits that look almost like ours.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a compelling narrative of temporal paradox and institutional horror. The author successfully captures the bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP entries, maintaining consistency throughout the technical containment procedures, object description, and exploration logs. The piece excels in its world-building and conceptual execution. The idea of doors leading to an archive containing records of a previous civilization that mirrors the current Foundation creates an effective sense of cosmic horror and institutional dread. The detail work is meticulous - from the specific measurements and damage patterns on the doors to the careful documentation of personnel matches across centuries. The exploration log section provides the strongest narrative momentum, with Team Theta-7's encounter with the hazmat-suited entities creating genuine tension. The description of the entities' impossible movement and their immediate flight upon detection adds an appropriately unsettling quality. The recovered documents are particularly effective, especially the fragment about being "archived" rather than destroyed, which reinforces the existential horror theme. However, the piece suffers from some structural issues. The middle sections become somewhat exposition-heavy, relying heavily on telling rather than showing. While appropriate for the SCP format, this creates periods where reader engagement dips. The characterization is necessarily limited by the clinical format, though the brief glimpses of the exploration team members feel authentic. The prose is generally clean and professional, avoiding purple prose while maintaining descriptive richness where needed. The temporal paradox elements are handled with appropriate ambiguity, never over-explaining the impossible aspects. The ending maintains the unsettling tone without forced resolution, which suits the SCP format well. The piece demonstrates creativity in its execution of familiar SCP tropes, particularly in the reversal of the Foundation's role from protector to potential repeat of a failed predecessor. The mounting evidence of O5 Council involvement adds layers of institutional paranoia that enhance the horror elements. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 12.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 8.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 8.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 84.3
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The gate of the Earth Embassy on Mireth did not open outward but slid downward into the basalt like a tongue retreating. Renn stepped through alone. Protocol forbade it. The briefing had been explicit: remain within the compound until the formal procession, observe only through sanctioned channels, do not—under any circumstance—walk the streets unescorted. The divergence of Mireth’s lineages was, according to Command, a tinderbox of ancient grievance and engineered separation. To wander was to risk not only the envoy’s safety but the fragile neutrality of the mission. Renn knew this. They went anyway. The air outside was thinner than in the compound’s filtered halls, carrying the scent of burnt sugar and something mineral, like rain on hot stone. The city rose in tiers of black basalt, every surface carved. Not with the utilitarian markings of Earth’s colonies, but with glyphs—old Earth myths rendered in patient, weathered relief. Prometheus chained to a rock, fire cupped in his hands like a stolen egg. Icarus falling, wings already melting, his face turned not in terror but in wonder. The Tower of Babel, its bricks scattering downward into separate tongues. And everywhere, the labyrinth, the minotaur waiting at its center. Renn walked slowly, letting gloved fingers trail the cool stone, wondering what the carvers had meant by preserving these particular stories. Hubris. Division. The cost of reaching too far from home. The market spilled through an archway beneath a relief of Babel’s collapse. Here, the official briefings dissolved like salt in water. Renn had expected segregation. The reports spoke of the Spire-Folk—tall, pale, elongated by generations in the high plateaus, their lungs adapted to thin air—and the Deep-Born, stocky and dark-eyed, bred for pressure and heat in the valley mines. Two subspecies, the documents insisted, locked in a cold war over water rights and genetic purity, each claiming sovereignty over the carved cities. But in the market, they mingled without ceremony. A Spire-Folk woman, silver-haired and seven feet of graceful bone, haggled with a Deep-Born vendor over bundles of dried root. Their children—some tall and slender, others broad and low—chased a shared toy through the crowd, laughing in a dialect that seemed to borrow from both lineages. Stalls sold woven cloth dyed with valley minerals and highland moss in patterns that neither culture claimed exclusively. An old man with Deep-Born shoulders and Spire-Folk eyes—mixed, unmistakably—sat playing a stringed instrument, singing a ballad about the minotaur not as monster but as lost brother. Renn bought nothing. They only watched, memorizing the contradictions: the shared language of trade, the intermarriage visible in faces, the absence of the armed tension Command had described. The divergence was real—bone structure, respiration, even the rhythm of heartbeats differed—but separation was not hatred. It was architecture. It was politics made flesh, perhaps, but not inevitable war. By the time Renn returned to the compound, the twin moons had risen. The gate slid open silently. They were not alone. Proctor Venn waited in the anteroom, arms crossed, face carved with the same severity as the basalt outside. “You were seen,” Venn said. Not a question. “I walked,” Renn admitted. “The brief said—” “The brief said remain. You disobeyed direct protocol in a contested zone.” “I observed—” “You endangered the mission.” Venn’s voice was flat, tired. “The Minister of Science has requested an audience with you. Not a courtesy. A command.” Renn felt the weight of it. “Minister Cael?” “The same. You will present yourself at the Observatory Spire within the hour. You will answer his questions fully. And you will understand that this is not a reward for your curiosity, envoy. It is a test.” The Observatory Spire was not an observatory. It was a political posting disguised as one—Renn knew this now—housed in a tower of black stone where glyphs of Prometheus and Babel repeated in spiraling bands. Minister Cael received them in a chamber lit by bioluminescent fungus, seated behind a table of polished obsidian. He was not young, nor old in any readable way; his features seemed assembled from both lineages, ambiguous, deliberate. He wore no insignia of science, only a simple dark coat. “You walked,” Cael said, without preamble. “Yes.” “Alone.” “Yes.” “And what did you see that our reports failed to show you?” Renn met his gaze. This was the test—not obedience, but judgment. To lie was to fail; to speak carelessly was to fail. “I saw integration,” Renn said. “Not conflict. The Spire-Folk and Deep-Born share markets, children, songs. The divergence is biological, but the separation is—maintained. By whom, I do not yet know. But the tension Command described does not exist in the streets. It exists in documents.” Cael leaned forward. His hands were long, elegant, resting on the obsidian like a musician’s. “Clever. You noticed the glyphs?” “The myths,” Renn said. “Prometheus. Babel. They warn of reaching and of division.” “They warn of hubris,” Cael corrected softly. “Of believing that separation is strength. Of engineering difference and calling it destiny. You are young, envoy. Offworld. You come from Earth where humanity still pretends to be one species, untouched by adaptation. You think you understand divergence because you have read files.” “I understand that I was sent to resolve a conflict that may be manufactured,” Renn said, their voice steady despite the tremor in their hands. “And I understand that you are not a scientist, Minister. You are a politician who knows the value of an envoy who sees clearly.” A smile touched Cael’s mouth, brief and sharp. “Good. You have wits. Do you have will? Because I will ask you now to choose. You may report back to your Command that Mireth is a powder keg, that the lineages are irreconcilable, that Earth must intervene with force or separation. That is the official narrative. It serves certain interests—both here and there.” Renn said nothing. “Or,” Cael continued, “you may report the truth. That the people are already one culture wearing two bodies. That the tension is fed by those who profit from division—the mining consortia, the genetic preservationists, perhaps even your own Command, which needs an enemy to justify its presence. If you report the truth, you will be recalled. Disgraced. Perhaps worse.” The chamber seemed to hold its breath. Renn thought of the market. The child with the toy. The old man singing. The glyphs of Babel, not as warning but as prophecy fulfilled—not in the falling away, but in the refusal to fall. “I will report what I saw,” Renn said. Cael studied them for a long moment. Then he stood, moving to the wall where the glyph of Prometheus was carved deepest. “You are tested, then. Not by me alone. By this world. The myths on these walls are not decorations, envoy. They are instructions. Prometheus stole fire and was chained for it—not because fire was wrong, but because he shared it with those who were meant to remain in darkness. Babel fell because they spoke one language and were divided by fear, not by tongue. You must decide whether you are here to maintain the chains, or to share the fire.” He turned back, his expression unreadable. “Go back to your quarters. Write your report. And know that if you choose truth, you choose solitude. The political operators—here and on Earth—do not forgive clarity.” Renn bowed, not in submission but in acknowledgment. As they left the spire, the air smelled again of burnt sugar and stone. The glyphs watched them go—Prometheus with his fire, Icarus falling upward, Babel’s bricks scattered not into chaos but into pattern. Renn walked back through the gate that slid like a tongue, carrying the weight of what they had seen, and the knowledge that the real diplomacy had only just begun.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Le Guin's style, successfully capturing her characteristic blend of anthropological observation, political complexity, and philosophical depth. The writing exhibits Le Guin's trademark restraint and precision, avoiding melodrama while building tension through careful observation and moral complexity. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece follows the diplomatic envoy's journey precisely as requested, incorporating all required elements: the unauthorized street walk, the encounter with ordinary citizens that contradicts briefings, the reprimand, and the challenging conversation with the Minister of Science. The 1000-word length is respected. The characterization is nuanced and believable. Renn acts with the curiosity and moral courage typical of Le Guin protagonists, while Minister Cael embodies the complex political operator described in the prompt. Both characters feel authentic rather than archetypal, with Cael particularly well-realized as someone who tests through indirection rather than confrontation. The world-building is exceptional, creating a believable human colony where divergence has occurred but integration persists despite political manipulation. The use of mythological glyphs (Prometheus, Babel, Icarus) as recurring motifs adds thematic depth without feeling forced. The Spire-Folk and Deep-Born are convincingly rendered as adapted human subspecies. The prose style successfully emulates Le Guin's voice - measured, observational, with moments of poetic precision. Sentences like "The gate of the Earth Embassy on Mireth did not open outward but slid downward into the basalt like a tongue retreating" demonstrate the author's control over rhythm and imagery. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and purposeful. Cael's testing of Renn through questions and philosophical challenges rings true to the political dynamics described. The conversation builds tension effectively without becoming overwrought. The piece shows rather than tells throughout most of its length, particularly in the market scene where the contradiction between official reports and reality is demonstrated through concrete observations rather than exposition. The ending avoids false positivity while maintaining hope - Renn faces genuine consequences for choosing truth, but the choice itself represents earned moral growth. The transformation feels organic rather than imposed. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the mythological symbolism borders on heavy-handedness, and some descriptions, while beautiful, occasionally lean toward the ornate. However, these elements remain within the bounds of sophisticated literary fiction rather than crossing into purple prose. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter: Generation Lock** The solar rig flickered. Not the gentle dimming of a cloud passing over the scavenged array—those cracked monocrystalline panels, wired with copper stripped from drone husks and held together with epoxy and prayer—but a violent, stuttering brownout. The voltage dropped, and *The Oven*—Vex’s jury-rigged thermal cycler, built from a salvaged 3D-printer heating bed, an Arduino clone, and copper tubing scavenged from a dead HVAC unit—hiccupped. The LED display, a repurposed digital thermometer, blinked from 94°C to 71°C and back, casting the container in sickly pulses. “Shit,” Vex said. Their hands—silver-veined, trembling only slightly—moved to the manual override. The physical toll was always worst when the power dipped. The modification—an untested splice of extremophile bacterial DNA and human mitochondrial repair sequences, administered six months ago in a moment of desperation—made Vex’s bones feel like wet chalk in low pressure. Their left eye wept a faint metallic sheen when the splice was wrong, and their joints ached with a deep, grinding familiarity. But there was the upside: they could see the color bands in the gel electrophoresis box without the UV lamp running. Their modified metabolism sang in the presence of pure chemicals. Their heart—spliced with an oxen ventricle—pumped with a strength that kept them standing when the brownouts came. The lab was a shipping container half-buried in the rubble of the old AgriTech arcology. Outside, the settlement—called The Root—grew what they could in toxic soil. Inside, Vex fought the corporate legacy: **Generation Lock**. Every seed AgriGen and BioDyne had released before the Collapse was designed with built-in obsolescence. Epigenetic kill-switches. Methylation bombs that silenced reproductive genes after one harvest. The DNA didn’t just fail; it unraveled. By the second generation, chromosomes sheared like wet paper. The corporations had engineered starvation into abundance, ensuring perpetual dependency—until the supply chains died and the seeds became useless relics. Vex was trying to fix that. On the workbench, vials of rapidly degrading genetic material glowed faintly in the unstable light. Corporate Soy-9, high-yield, protein-dense, and already half-decayed in the preservative solution. Wild-type rice genome, salvaged from a pre-Modification seed bank in a flooded basement. The splice was delicate: excising the Termination Sequence without destabilizing the yield chromosomes. The equipment was all salvage. The centrifuge was a washing-machine motor bolted to a steel drum, balanced with lead weights. The pipettes were glass syringes with rubber bulbs from a looted medical kit, calibrated by eye and instinct. The microscope was a USB endoscope taped to a magnifying glass stand, feeding to a cracked tablet. Everything smelled of ethanol—stolen, distilled, precious—and ozone. Vex adjusted the solar regulator—a hacked smart-meter and a car battery that held charge like a sieve. The panels outside flickered again, casting strobing shadows across the container walls. Then the knock came. Not the settlement’s knock—three quick, two slow. This was one sharp rap, followed by silence. Vex didn’t look up immediately. They finished transferring the plasmid into the thermal cycler, locking the tube with a click that felt like closing a coffin. Only then did they turn. The door—reinforced with rebar—opened a crack. Moss slid in. Moss was a broker. Their eyes were wrong: too clear, too reflective, likely spliced with corporate night-vision retroviruses never meant for human use. They wore a coat of synthetic leather that refused to biodegrade—another corporate leftover. “You’re running the rig hard,” Moss said, voice like dry leaves. “I have to,” Vex said. “The settlement needs the wheat splice by dawn. The old stock’s failing.” Moss placed a case on the bench. Aluminum, dented, with a biometric lock that had been forced open and rewired. “That’s not why I’m here. This is a separate contract.” Vex’s modified heart skipped. “I don’t take outside work during planting season.” “This isn’t planting. This is extraction.” Moss opened the case. Inside, nested in foam that smelled of chemicals, was a single vial. The liquid inside was black, iridescent, moving with a viscosity that defied gravity. “Corporate source,” Moss said. “Pre-Collapse. A strain of rice engineered not for yield, but for alkaloid production. Pain suppression. Euphoria. The DNA is degrading faster than usual—accelerated obsolescence, maybe a failed prototype. I need it stabilized. Spliced into a viable host. Wild-type wheat, your settlement’s strain. I need it to grow.” Vex stared. “That’s not food. That’s narcotic agriculture.” “It’s medicine,” Moss corrected. “Black market medicine. The settlement’s healer is running out of synthesized opioids. This is sustainable. One harvest, and they have a year’s supply. But the DNA is falling apart. I need your splice.” “And the payment?” Moss slid a second container across the bench. Inside: pure restriction enzymes, uncut, in crystalline suspension. And a voltage regulator—industrial grade, pre-Collapse, capable of smoothing the solar rig into steady current. Vex’s hands ached. The physical toll always spiked when they were stressed—joints locking, silver veins pulsing visibly beneath the skin. But there was the upside: they could taste the absence of corporate tracking markers in the enzymes. They could feel the chemical purity like a chord struck in their chest. “If I do this,” Vex said slowly, “the wheat becomes a vector. The settlement eats it by accident—” “Then don’t let them. Label it. Control it. You’re already splicing for them. This just pays for the equipment to keep doing it.” The solar panels flickered violently. *The Oven*’s display went dark, then flickered back—82°C, unstable. Vex made a decision. They took the black vial. The liquid inside seemed to watch them, synthetic intelligence embedded in nucleotides. “I’ll need four hours,” Vex said. “And I keep the second-generation seeds. For the settlement.” Moss smiled, too sharp. “Contract is for one harvest. You keep the seeds, I don’t care. Just make it grow.” As Moss left, Vex turned back to the bench. They injected the black alkaloid sequence into the wheat plasmid, using the salvaged pipette with hands that shouldn’t have been steady but were—thanks to the medicine, thanks to the spliced oxen heart and bacterial repair mechanisms that made them something other than human, something useful. The physical toll hit then: a wave of nausea, silver blood welling in the corner of their left eye—the eye that wept metal when the splice was wrong. But also the upside: clarity. They saw the DNA strands in their mind’s eye, the broken corporate chains, the wild-type strength they were weaving in. Outside, the solar rig flickered, then steadied—just barely—as the car battery took the load. Inside, *The Oven* hummed, reaching temperature. Vex worked through the brownout, splicing the future of The Root into something that could survive, even if it was built on poison. The contract was done. The black market had paid in power and purity. And Vex, silver-veined and trembling, smiled. The seeds would grow. For one generation. And then, if they were careful, for another.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk chapter demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering with built-in obsolescence has led to societal collapse. The author effectively incorporates all requested elements: jury-rigged lab equipment, physical tolls and benefits of genetic modifications, and a black market contract as the central plot device. The worldbuilding is particularly strong, with vivid technical details that feel authentic to the genre. The description of "The Oven" thermal cycler built from salvaged parts, the washing-machine centrifuge, and the solar rig creates a convincing picture of resourceful survival. The concept of "Generation Lock" - DNA designed to fail after one harvest - is a compelling central premise that drives both the worldbuilding and character motivation. Vex is a well-developed protagonist whose genetic modifications create interesting physical consequences - the silver veins, metallic tears, and enhanced sensory abilities. These modifications feel integrated into both their character and the world rather than superficial additions. The character's moral complexity is evident in their willingness to create narcotic crops for medical purposes while maintaining concern for their settlement's safety. The prose maintains a consistent dystopian tone throughout, with technical language that enhances rather than obscures the narrative. The writing effectively balances exposition with action, though it occasionally leans toward the technical side. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and serves the plot effectively. The black market contract provides solid narrative tension and raises genuine moral questions about survival versus ethics. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither overly optimistic nor gratuitously dark - reflecting the pragmatic compromises necessary in this world. Some minor weaknesses include occasional over-description of technical details that could slow pacing, and the character of Moss feels somewhat archetypal as the mysterious broker figure. The resolution, while satisfying, follows a fairly predictable path once the contract is introduced.
